title: Cat Loss of Appetite for 24 Hours: When to Worry and What to Do
meta_desc: Is your cat refusing food? Learn why a 24-hour loss of appetite is a concern, when it becomes an emergency, and how a triage photo can help.
content_html:
What is Anorexia in Cats?

When a cat stops eating for 24 hours, it is medically referred to as anorexia. Unlike humans or even dogs, cats have a unique metabolism that requires consistent protein and energy intake. A total lack of food intake for a full day is a signal that something is wrong, ranging from dental pain to underlying systemic illness.

Is it an Emergency?

Urgency Level: Medium. While not always an immediate life-threatening crisis in the first 24 hours, it is a situation that requires prompt attention. If your cat is also lethargic, vomiting, or hiding, the urgency increases. Cats are prone to developing hepatic lipidosis (fatty liver disease) if they go too long without eating, which can become life-threatening very quickly.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Sharing a photo of your cat with a veterinary professional can be incredibly helpful for triage. A specialist can look for signs of 'pain face' (grimace scale), unusual posture, or changes in the eyes and coat. A video of them sniffing food and turning away can also help determine if the issue is nausea or a physical difficulty with chewing.

faqs.question: Can I wait another day to see if my cat's appetite returns?
faqs.answer: It is generally not recommended to wait beyond 24 hours. Cats, especially those who are overweight, can begin to develop liver issues after only a short period of fasting.

faqs.question: What are the most common reasons a cat stops eating?
faqs.answer: Common causes include dental disease, kidney issues, gastrointestinal upset, respiratory infections that block their sense of smell, or even stress from changes in the household.

faqs.question: Should I try to force-feed my cat?
faqs.answer: You should never force-feed a cat without veterinary guidance, as this can cause food aversion or accidental aspiration into the lungs. Always consult a professional first.
